report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Jess, thanks for sharing all of this with us. A few clear patterns stand out that may explain why you feel like you've lost touch with the version of yourself that used to have so much energy.

### The Dieting Cycle Is Working Against You

You mentioned that you've lost weight before through calorie counting and intermittent fasting, but it always comes back. This is one of the most well-documented patterns in metabolic research. When the body faces repeated caloric restriction, it [responds by conserving energy, lowering metabolic rate disproportionately to the weight lost](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11154-025-09970-9) and suppressing thyroid axis function. Intermittent fasting specifically has been shown to [downregulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10005667/), the very system that controls your metabolic rate. Each cycle of restriction may have lowered your baseline metabolism further, making regain almost inevitable and leaving you with less energy than you started with. This isn't a failure of willpower. It's your biology adapting to perceived scarcity.

### A Gut-Hormone Connection Worth Exploring

You're experiencing digestive issues alongside puffiness and water retention, and those two things are more connected than they might seem. The gut microbiome directly influences estrogen levels through what's called the estrobolome, a collection of bacteria that regulate how estrogen is processed. When the gut is out of balance, [this system can become disrupted, altering circulating estrogen levels](https://mail.mattioli1885journals.com/index.php/actabiomedica/article/view/15980) and contributing to water retention, difficulty keeping weight off, and even the fatigue you're describing. Your digestive symptoms suggest this connection is worth a closer look.

### High Stress as a Hidden Metabolic Brake

You noted high stress, and while your sleep seems okay for now, chronic stress quietly undermines metabolism in ways that compound over time. Elevated cortisol promotes fat storage (especially when combined with the metabolic suppression from dieting), increases gut permeability, and drains key nutrients like magnesium and vitamin C that your body needs to produce energy efficiently. Stress may also be contributing to your digestive issues, since it directly impairs gut motility and immune function.

### The Energy Paradox

You rated your energy at 8/10 but also said you're "always tired" and want to feel like yourself again. This kind of gap is common when someone has gradually adapted to a lower baseline. What used to feel like tiredness now feels normal, and "good energy" is relative to a new, diminished standard. The combination of repeated dieting, high stress, and digestive dysfunction can quietly erode metabolic output without a single dramatic crash.

---

These patterns are interconnected, and that's actually encouraging. It means that addressing the right underlying factors could shift multiple things at once. A quiz like this can only surface so much, and there's more to your story worth exploring, but what you've shared already paints a coherent picture of what might be going on beneath the surface.
